content:
# Leviticus Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59r–59v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_004_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_004_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 88–89 (pa_0088–pa_0089)

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: If any person sins unintentionally in any of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done, and does any one of them —

**3** if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people — then he shall bring to the LORD for the sin that he has committed a bull from the herd without blemish as a sin offering.

**4** He shall bring the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting before the LORD, and he shall lay his hand on the head of the bull and slaughter the bull before the LORD.

**5** Then the anointed priest shall take some of the blood of the bull and bring it to the tent of meeting.

**6** And the priest shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle some of the blood seven times before the LORD before the veil of the sanctuary.

**7** And the priest shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar of fragrant incense that is before the LORD in the tent of meeting, and all the rest of the blood of the bull he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering that is at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**8** And all the fat of the bull of the sin offering he shall remove from it: the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails,

**9** and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys —

**10** just as it is taken from the ox of the sacrifice of peace offerings — and the priest shall burn them on the altar of burnt offering.

**11** But the skin of the bull, and all its flesh, together with its head and its legs and its entrails and its dung —

**12** the whole bull he shall carry outside the camp to a clean place, to the place where the ashes are poured out, and he shall burn it on a wood fire. At the place where the ashes are poured out it shall be burned.

**13** And if the whole congregation of Israel sins unintentionally and the thing is hidden from the eyes of the assembly, and they do any one of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done, and they bear guilt —

**14** when the sin which they have committed becomes known, the assembly shall offer a bull from the herd as a sin offering and bring it before the tent of meeting.

**15** And the elders of the congregation shall lay their hands on the head of the bull before the LORD, and the bull shall be slaughtered before the LORD.

**16** Then the anointed priest shall bring some of the blood of the bull to the tent of meeting,

**17** and the priest shall dip his finger in some of the blood and sprinkle it seven times before the LORD before the veil.

**18** And he shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar that is before the LORD in the tent of meeting, and all the rest of the blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering that is at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**19** And all its fat he shall remove from it and burn on the altar.

**20** He shall do with the bull just as he did with the bull of the sin offering; so shall he do with it. And the priest shall make atonement for them, and they shall be forgiven.

**21** And he shall carry the bull outside the camp and burn it as he burned the first bull; it is the sin offering for the assembly.

**22** When a leader sins, doing unintentionally any one of all the things the LORD his God has commanded not to be done, and he becomes guilty —

**23** when his sin which he has committed is made known to him, he shall bring as his offering a goat, a male without blemish.

**24** And he shall lay his hand on the head of the goat and slaughter it in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering before the LORD; it is a sin offering.

**25** Then the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering.

**26** And all its fat he shall burn on the altar, as the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings. So the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin, and he shall be forgiven.

**27** If any one of the common people sins unintentionally in doing any one of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done, and he becomes guilty —

**28** when his sin which he has committed is made known to him, he shall bring as his offering a goat, a female without blemish, for his sin which he has committed.

**29** And he shall lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and slaughter the sin offering in the place of the burnt offering.

**30** Then the priest shall take some of its blood with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and all its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar.

**31** And all its fat he shall remove, as the fat is removed from the sacrifice of peace offerings, and the priest shall burn it on the altar for a pleasing aroma to the LORD. So the priest shall make atonement for him, and he shall be forgiven.

**32** If he brings a lamb as his offering for a sin offering, he shall bring a female without blemish.

**33** And he shall lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and slaughter it for a sin offering in the place where they slaughter the burnt offering.

**34** Then the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and all its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar.

**35** And all its fat he shall remove as the fat of the lamb is removed from the sacrifice of peace offerings, and the priest shall burn it on the altar on the fire offerings of the LORD. So the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin which he has committed, and he shall be forgiven.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59r–59v.** Both pages of the Leningrad Codex were examined directly. The manuscript images show clear three-column layouts in good condition throughout. Folio 59r opens the chapter immediately following the peace offering legislation of chapter 3. The chapter heading area shows the standard Masoretic formatting, with the parasha division marking the shift from the shelamim legislation to the sin offering (chattat) legislation — a major structural pivot in the Levitical cult manual. The characteristic marker וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה (va-yedabber YHWH el-Mosheh, "and the LORD spoke to Moses") is clearly visible at the chapter opening.

The four-case structure of the chapter is visible as four distinct legislative paragraphs in the manuscript: the anointed priest (vv. 3–12), the congregation (vv. 13–21), the leader/nasi (vv. 22–26), and the common person (vv. 27–35). The repetition of the structural formula וְנִסְלַח לוֹ ("and he shall be forgiven") at the conclusion of the leader and common person sections (vv. 26, 31, 35) is a visually prominent feature of the closing verses.

The key term bi-shgagah ("unintentionally," vv. 2, 13, 22, 27) appears at the opening of each case and is rendered consistently. The blood-manipulation distinctions between the cases are reflected in precise vocabulary in the manuscript: ve-hizzah ("and he shall sprinkle," vv. 6, 17) for the priest/congregation cases — involving sevenfold sprinkling before the veil — versus ve-natan ("and he shall put," vv. 25, 30, 34) for the leader/commoner cases — involving only the horns of the altar of burnt offering. This lexical distinction is preserved in the translation ("sprinkle" vs. "put").

Folio 59v carries the conclusion of the chapter (from approximately v. 22 onward) through v. 35. The text of vv. 27–35 (the common person section) is fully legible, including the alternative provision of a lamb (vv. 32–35) as well as the goat (vv. 27–31). The word chattat ("sin offering") appears repeatedly in the final columns, visually dominating the close of the chapter.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 88–89 (LXX, pa_0088–pa_0089).** Page 88 (pa_0088) and page 89 (pa_0089) were examined directly. Page 88 continues from the end of the chapter 3 material visible at the top of the column into the full LXX text of Leviticus 4. The uncial Greek text proceeds in three columns without word division, in clear fourth-century hand. The chapter numeration "CAP. XXXII" (or its equivalent in the LXX numbering scheme) is visible in the outer margin. Page 89 carries the text through the conclusion of the chapter. Key LXX lexical choices for the sin offering legislation are noted in the variants table below.

The LXX (Vaticanus) shows no major textual dislocations in this chapter relative to the MT. The four-case structure is preserved intact. The most significant divergences are lexical: the rendering of chattat as περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("concerning sin" / "for sin"), the consistent use of ἐξιλάσεται ("shall make propitiation/atonement") for kipper, and the use of ἀφεθήσεται ("shall be forgiven") for nishlach, all of which are in substantial agreement with the MT. The sprinkling verb nazah / hizzah in vv. 6 and 17 is rendered προσρανεῖ ("shall sprinkle") in the LXX, confirming the MT's distinction from the simple "put" (natan) of vv. 25, 30, 34.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Bi-shgagah / "unintentionally" (vv. 2, 13, 22, 27).** Hebrew בִּשְׁגָגָה (bi-shgagah) is the governing concept of the entire chapter. The root שגג (shagag) denotes going astray, erring, or stumbling — specifically the inadvertent, unwitting transgression as opposed to deliberate, high-handed rebellion (which the sin offering cannot atone for; cf. Num 15:30). The chapter's sin offering (chattat) is precisely calibrated for this category of sin: the person who commits a violation of a divine command unknowingly, or without full awareness, and later discovers the fact.

   "Unintentionally" (ESV, NASB, NIV) is the standard modern rendering and is retained here. "Inadvertently" (NJPS) is a precise alternative. "Through error" (older KJV tradition) is overly narrow. The LXX renders ἀκουσίως ("involuntarily, unwittingly"), which is a close semantic equivalent preserving the absence-of-deliberate-will aspect. The theological import is that unintentional sin is still real sin requiring atonement — the offering is not merely a formality but a genuine cultic remedy for a genuine moral/legal violation.

2. **Chattat / "sin offering" (throughout).** Hebrew חַטָּאת (chattat) is the name of this entire class of sacrifice. The root חטא (chata') denotes "missing the mark" or "falling short." In the Levitical sacrificial system the chattat is specifically the offering that addresses the defilement caused by sin — the unwitting violation that has nonetheless polluted the offerer and, in the case of the priest or congregation, the sanctuary itself. This translation renders chattat as "sin offering" throughout (ESV, NASB, KJV), which is the established formal equivalence rendering. "Purification offering" (Milgrom, NJPS) is a defensible alternative that emphasizes the purificatory function of the blood ritual — the blood cleanses the sancta rather than (only) atoning for the person — but "sin offering" is retained for consistency with established Leviticus-series conventions and liturgical familiarity. The LXX renders περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("concerning/for sin"), which is a periphrastic phrase rather than a single technical term.

3. **Kohen ha-mashiach / "the anointed priest" (vv. 3, 5, 16).** Hebrew הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ (ha-kohen ha-mashiach) is the first appearance in the Pentateuch of the term mashiach (from mashach, "to anoint") as a title for a specific individual official. Here it designates the high priest — the one anointed with the special anointing oil of Exodus 30 — who, by virtue of his singular consecrated status, occupies the highest-ranked position in the sin offering hierarchy. His sin, unlike that of any other individual, potentially implicates the entire people in guilt (v. 3: "bringing guilt on the people").

   "The anointed priest" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB) and is used here. The LXX renders ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός ("the priest, the anointed one" / "the anointed priest"), translating mashiach with χριστός (christos), the Greek word that in its later New Testament usage becomes "Christ." This is the earliest occurrence of the Hebrew-Greek christological vocabulary in Leviticus, though here it is purely a priestly-cultic title. No messianic interpretation is imported; the translation renders it "the anointed priest" as a title for the high priestly office.

4. **Nasi / "leader" (vv. 22–26).** Hebrew נָשִׂיא (nasi) is a term for a tribal or clan chieftain — a person of recognized community authority below the level of the entire congregation. "Leader" (ESV, NASB) is the standard rendering and is used here. "Ruler" (KJV) and "chieftain" (NJPS) are alternatives. The nasi's offering is a male goat (se'ir izzim, "a goat of the goats," i.e., a male goat), significantly lesser than the bull required for the priest or congregation — a graduated scale reflecting ranked culpability and social position. The LXX renders ἄρχων ("ruler, leader"), a close semantic equivalent.

5. **Ve-hizzah / ve-natan — "sprinkle" vs. "put" (vv. 6–7 vs. 25, 30, 34).** The chapter contains one of the most theologically significant distinctions in Leviticus: the difference in blood manipulation between the upper-tier cases (anointed priest, vv. 5–7; congregation, vv. 16–18) and the lower-tier cases (nasi, v. 25; common person, vv. 30, 34). For the priest and congregation, the blood is (a) brought into the tent of meeting, (b) sprinkled (nazah / hizzah — "sprinkle, spatter") seven times before the veil of the sanctuary, and (c) applied to the horns of the inner altar of incense. For the nasi and common person, the blood is (a) not brought inside the tent, (b) not sprinkled before the veil, and (c) applied only to the horns of the outer altar of burnt offering.

   The distinction turns on two different Hebrew verbs: הִזָּה (hizzah, hiphil of נזה, nazah — "to sprinkle, to spatter with drops") in vv. 6 and 17, versus נָתַן (natan — "to put, to place") in vv. 25, 30, 34. The sevenfold sprinkling (sheva pe'amim) is reserved for the cases where the sanctuary itself has been defiled (priest/congregation) and requires inner-sanctum purification. The translation renders hizzah as "sprinkle" and natan as "put" throughout, preserving the ritual and spatial distinction. The LXX renders hizzah with προσρανεῖ ("shall sprinkle") and natan with ἐπιθήσει ("shall place/put"), in close agreement.

6. **Parokhet ha-qodesh / "the veil of the sanctuary" (vv. 6, 17).** Hebrew פָּרֹכֶת הַקֹּדֶשׁ (parokhet ha-qodesh) designates the curtain or veil that separates the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place (the Holy of Holies). In vv. 6 and 17 the anointed priest sprinkles blood "before the LORD before the veil of the sanctuary" — i.e., facing the inner curtain that screens the Ark of the Covenant. This is a spatially precise act of purification: the priest is in the Holy Place, facing the veil, addressing the defilement of the sanctuary at its inner boundary without entering the Most Holy Place.

   "The veil of the sanctuary" renders parokhet ha-qodesh literally (ESV: "the veil of the sanctuary"; NASB: "the veil of the sanctuary"). "The curtain of the sanctuary" is equally valid. This translation retains "veil" as the established formal term for the parokhet. The LXX renders τὸ καταπέτασμα τοῦ ἁγίου ("the curtain/veil of the holy [place]"), a close equivalent; καταπέτασμα is the standard LXX term for parokhet throughout.

7. **"The altar of fragrant incense" (v. 7) vs. "the altar of burnt offering" (vv. 7, 10, 18, 25, 30, 34).** The chapter carefully distinguishes between two altars: (a) the inner altar, the "altar of fragrant incense" (mizbeach ha-qetoret ha-sammim) located in the Holy Place before the veil, and (b) the outer altar, the "altar of burnt offering" (mizbeach ha-olah) at the entrance of the tent. For the upper-tier cases (priest/congregation), blood is applied to the horns of the inner incense altar AND excess blood is poured at the base of the outer burnt offering altar. For the lower-tier cases (nasi/commoner), blood is applied only to the horns of the outer burnt offering altar.

   This two-altar distinction is fundamental to the spatial theology of the Levitical sanctuary. The inner altar is closer to the divine presence; its blood application signals that the priest's/congregation's sin has defiled the inner sanctum and must be addressed there. "The altar of fragrant incense" (v. 7) follows the ESV and NASB in rendering mizbeach qetoret ha-sammim fully. The LXX renders τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου τοῦ θυμιάματος τῆς συνθέσεως ("the altar of the compound incense"), which is even more specific, reflecting Exod 30's "blended/compound incense" (qetoret sammim).

8. **"At the base of the altar" (vv. 7, 18, 25, 30, 34).** Hebrew אֶל-יְסוֹד מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה (el-yesod mizbeach ha-olah) — "at/to the base of the altar of burnt offering." The yesod ("base, foundation") of the altar is the lowest structural element at ground level, where excess blood was collected. Pouring the blood at the base of the altar completes the blood disposal and ensures no blood is wasted or profaned. The translation renders yesod as "base" throughout, consistent with ESV/NASB. The LXX renders τὸν θεμέλιον ("the foundation"), in close agreement.

9. **"Becomes known" / "is made known to him" (vv. 14, 23, 28).** Hebrew וְנוֹדְעָה (ve-noda'ah, niphal of ידע, "and it becomes known") in v. 14, and אוֹ הוֹדַע אֵלָיו חַטָּאתוֹ (o hoda' elav chattat'o, "or his sin is made known to him") in vv. 23 and 28. These phrases describe the triggering moment for the sin offering: the moment when the previously unknown sin comes to light — either discovered by the community (congregation case) or revealed to the individual (nasi/commoner cases). The rendering "becomes known" (v. 14) and "is made known to him" (vv. 23, 28) preserves the passive/reflexive Niphal-Hophal distinction in the MT and reflects the significance of the moment of moral/legal discovery as the cultic trigger.

10. **"And they shall be forgiven" / "and he shall be forgiven" (vv. 20, 26, 31, 35).** Hebrew וְנִסְלַח לָהֶם / לוֹ (ve-nishlach lahem / lo, niphal of סלח, "and it shall be forgiven to them/him"). The Niphal of salach is used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible — God is always the subject of salach; only God forgives. "They/he shall be forgiven" is the standard formal rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV). The LXX renders ἀφεθήσεται αὐτοῖς / αὐτῷ ("it shall be forgiven to them/him"), using the future passive of ἀφίημι — close agreement in both the passive construction and the theological claim that forgiveness is a divine act received, not a human achievement.

11. **"And the priest shall make atonement for him/them" (vv. 20, 26, 31, 35).** Hebrew וְכִפֶּר עֲלֵיהֶם / עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן (ve-khipper aleihem / alav ha-kohen, piel of כפר, kipper). The piel kipper is the central verb of the entire sacrificial theology of Leviticus: "to make atonement, to cover, to cleanse from guilt." The priest is the agent; the LORD is implicitly the one satisfied; the offerer is the beneficiary. "Make atonement" is the established formal rendering (Lev 1:4 note). The LXX renders ἐξιλάσεται ("shall propitiate, make expiation"), from ἱλάσκομαι, which has a connotation of appeasing or propitiating a deity. While the Hebrew kipper does not necessarily carry the propitiation nuance — it may be more about purification — the LXX rendering reflects a genuine interpretive tradition. This translation follows the MT rendering "make atonement" rather than the LXX's more propitiation-weighted term.

12. **"The whole congregation of Israel" (v. 13).** Hebrew כָּל-עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל (kol-adat Yisrael) — "the whole congregation/assembly of Israel." The 'edah is the assembled covenantal community as a corporate entity distinct from individual members; its collective sin demands a collective remedy. "Congregation" (ESV, KJV) is used rather than "community" (NJPS) or "assembly" (NIV) to reflect the cultic/covenantal gathering sense of 'edah. "The whole congregation of Israel" preserves the full weight of kol as a collective modifier.

13. **"A clean place" / "the place where the ashes are poured out" (v. 12).** Hebrew אֶל-מָקוֹם טָהוֹר (el-maqom tahor, "to a clean place") and אֶל-שֶׁפֶךְ הַדֶּשֶׁן (el-shephekh ha-deshen, "to the place of the ash-pouring / the ash heap"). The bull carcass of the priest's sin offering is taken outside the camp to a designated ash dump that is nonetheless declared "clean" — not ritually impure, despite its proximity to waste material. The contrast is significant: the offering is too holy for casual disposal (it cannot remain inside the camp) but goes to a clean place, not an unclean one. "A clean place" and "the place where the ashes are poured out" render both phrases literally. The LXX renders εἰς τόπον καθαρόν ("to a clean place") and εἰς τὴν ἐκχύσιν τῆς σποδοῦ ("to the outpouring place of the ashes"), in close agreement with the MT.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 88–89, pa_0088–pa_0089) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4:2 | bi-shgagah ("unintentionally") | ἀκουσίως ("involuntarily / unwittingly") | LXX renders the root accurately with ἀκουσίως (from ἄκων, "against one's will / unwittingly"). Close semantic agreement; LXX emphasizes absence of deliberate will. |
| 4:3 | kohen ha-mashiach ("the anointed priest") | ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός ("the priest, the anointed") | LXX uses χριστός (christos) for mashiach — the Greek term that becomes "Christ." Here purely a priestly-cultic title. The phrase "bringing guilt on the people" (le-asham ha-am) is rendered εἰς ἁμαρτίαν τῷ λαῷ ("for/as sin to the people") — close agreement. |
| 4:3 | chattat ("sin offering") | περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ("concerning sin / for sin") | LXX consistently renders chattat as the prepositional phrase περὶ ἁμαρτίας throughout the chapter, rather than a single technical term. This is the standard LXX translation of chattat and is consistent throughout Lev 4. |
| 4:6 | ve-hizzah ("and he shall sprinkle") | προσρανεῖ ("shall sprinkle") | LXX uses προσρανεῖ from προσραίνω ("to sprinkle toward/upon"), accurately rendering the directional sprinkling of hizzah. Full agreement on the sevenfold sprinkling. |
| 4:7 | mizbeach qetoret ha-sammim ("altar of fragrant incense") | τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου τοῦ θυμιάματος τῆς συνθέσεως ("altar of the compound/blended incense") | LXX adds τῆς συνθέσεως ("of the compound"), reflecting the Exod 30 specification that the incense is a blended preparation. The MT here simply has qetoret ha-sammim; LXX adds specificity absent from the MT at this point. |
| 4:12 | el-maqom tahor ("to a clean place") | εἰς τόπον καθαρόν ("to a clean place") | Direct agreement. |
| 4:12 | el-shephekh ha-deshen ("to the ash-heap / place of ash-pouring") | εἰς τὴν ἐκχύσιν τῆς σποδοῦ ("to the outpouring of the ashes") | LXX renders shephekh (a noun from shapakh, "to pour out") with ἐκχύσιν ("outpouring"), closely matching the MT's root semantics. |
| 4:13 | kol-adat Yisrael ("whole congregation of Israel") | πᾶσα συναγωγὴ Ισραηλ ("whole synagogue/assembly of Israel") | LXX uses συναγωγή ("synagogue/assembly") for 'edah, its standard rendering. Full agreement on kol ("whole/all"). |
| 4:13 | ve-ne'elam ("and it is hidden") | καὶ λάθῃ ("and it escapes notice") | LXX renders ne'elam (niphal of alam, "to be concealed") with λάθῃ (from λανθάνω, "to escape notice, to be undetected"). Close semantic agreement; both describe the congregation's sin as undetected. |
| 4:20 | ve-nishlach lahem ("and they shall be forgiven") | ἀφεθήσεται αὐτοῖς ("it shall be forgiven to them") | LXX renders the niphal salach with the future passive of ἀφίημι ("to release, forgive"), in close agreement. The passive construction in both languages preserves the theological point that forgiveness is received from God. |
| 4:22 | nasi ("leader") | ἄρχων ("ruler / leader") | LXX renders nasi with ἄρχων, a general term for a ruler or leader. Close agreement. The phrase "the LORD his God" (YHWH Elohav) is rendered Κύριος ὁ θεὸς αὐτοῦ, in direct agreement. |
| 4:23 | se'ir izzim ("a goat / male goat") | χίμαρον ἐξ αἰγῶν ἄρσεν ἄμωμον ("a male goat from the she-goats, without blemish") | LXX expands slightly, making explicit the gender (ἄρσεν, "male") and quality (ἄμωμον, "without blemish") that are implicit in the MT's se'ir. The MT specifies "male" only in the parallel lamb passage; LXX standardizes across both cases. This translation follows the MT's level of explicitness. |
| 4:25 | ve-natan ("and he shall put") | ἐπιθήσει ("shall place/put upon") | LXX uses ἐπιθήσει ("shall place upon") for natan ("put"), correctly distinguishing this from the sprinkling verb προσρανεῖ used in vv. 6 and 17 for the upper-tier cases. The blood-manipulation distinction is preserved in both MT and LXX. |
| 4:31 | le-re'ach nichoach la-YHWH ("for a pleasing aroma to the LORD") | εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας Κυρίῳ ("for a fragrance of sweet smell to the LORD") | LXX renders the anthropomorphic pleasing-aroma formula in the same terms as earlier chapters (ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας). Full agreement. Notably this formula appears only in the common person's case (v. 31) in ch. 4, not in the priest/congregation cases — and the LXX preserves this distribution exactly. |
| 4:35 | al-isheh ha-YHWH ("on the fire offerings of the LORD") | ἐπὶ τὰ κάρπωμα Κυρίου ("upon the burnt offerings of the LORD") | LXX renders isheh as κάρπωμα ("burnt offering / gift offering"), its standard rendering for isheh throughout Leviticus. See also Lev 3:11 note. LXX avoids the specific fire-derivation of isheh. This translation retains "fire offerings of the LORD." |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 4)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | The offering for unintentional sin; "purification offering" (Milgrom) is a defensible alternative; "sin offering" retained for series consistency |
| בִּשְׁגָגָה (bi-shgagah) | unintentionally | Unwitting violation as opposed to deliberate high-handed sin; LXX: ἀκουσίως |
| הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ (ha-kohen ha-mashiach) | the anointed priest | LXX renders χριστός — earliest christological vocabulary in Leviticus, here purely cultic |
| נָשִׂיא (nasi) | leader | Tribal/clan chieftain; LXX: ἄρχων |
| הִזָּה (hizzah) | sprinkle | Sevenfold before the veil — upper-tier cases (priest/congregation) only; distinct from "put" (natan) |
| נָתַן (natan, cultic blood) | put | Blood on altar horns — lower-tier cases (leader/commoner); distinct from sevenfold sprinkling |
| פָּרֹכֶת הַקֹּדֶשׁ (parokhet ha-qodesh) | veil of the sanctuary | Curtain separating Holy Place from Most Holy Place; LXX: καταπέτασμα τοῦ ἁγίου |
| וְנִסְלַח (ve-nishlach) | and he/they shall be forgiven | Niphal salach — divine forgiveness received; God always the implied subject of salach |
| כִּפֶּר (kipper) | make atonement | Piel; central Levitical atonement verb; LXX: ἐξιλάσεται |
| יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (yesod ha-mizbeach) | base of the altar | Where excess blood is poured; LXX: θεμέλιον |
| מָקוֹם טָהוֹר (maqom tahor) | clean place | Outside the camp — designated clean ash dump |
| עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל ('adat Yisrael) | congregation of Israel | Covenantal assembly as corporate entity; LXX: συναγωγή |
| זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים (zevach shelamim) | sacrifice of peace offerings | From Lev 3; continued reference point for fat-removal analogy |
| חֵלֶב (chelev) | fat | Internal suet; belongs exclusively to the LORD |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | pleasing aroma | Anthropomorphic idiom; appears only in the common person's case (v. 31) in this chapter |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |

— LOGOS
